author: @localbroadcast
text:
Allow me to split from the pack and insert my own bit of information to chew on... Can i suggest some type of boundary layer photon absorption phenomenon going on here?  Basically.. The size of a boundary layer between a hydrophobic material in water and the dissolved solids in water will increase or decrease in thickness depending on the amount of radiant energy being absorbed at that moment by the hydrophobic material... the effect can be seen to cause water to be pumped thru a narrow channel between 2 layers of such material.  The radiant energy that is absorbed causes a static charge on the material that opposes  the charge of the dissolved solids and keeps them further away depending on the amount of charge.  They proved that its caused by radiant energy because they isolated the experiment from all sources of outside radiant energy, be it from heat, light, whatever, and the effect of the boundary layer all but disapeared.  Obviously not all radiant energy could be blocked out... Everything always has a certain level of molecular motion(heat).
So.. instaed of having 2 layers of material close togethre and pumping water because of a static charge because of radiant energy... you are pumping the water at a different rate than what naturally would be the effect, and thus are generating a greater or lesser charge on the material because of this.  Did that make any sense?  I wish I could find the paper I read that described this phenomenon... But really this makes sense, and if you can nail this down as what the actual effect is... you can design your system accordingly to take advantage of it.  u know this already tho dont you! haha
good luck!

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: +localbroadcast you're talking about EZ depletion zones? - interesting idea mate

author: @simonmitchell
text: Hi Robert. My head is exploding with the notion that salt water over graphene can generate electricity. Is anybody exploring the idea of an ocean generator?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: i don't think so mate - the money needed to research this properly is too much for people I think

author: @TheGEODEAHOLIC
text: got to wonder if it could potentially be used for cathodic protection or for anti-fouling protection of ships hulls.  also sounds like one could spray a strip of plastic insulation on a hull, & then your waterproof ink, & have a poorboy electric generator.  add additional layersof plastic & paint & you might git a generator/capacitor combo... sometimes being hundreds of miles from anything much bigger then a bathtub is inconvienient.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: nice ideas mate - i will have a think about them

replies.author: @simonmitchell
replies.text: I'm exploring many different ideas on electricity generation, because let's face it - we can't rely on oil for much longer, and the idea that you could coat a ships hull in a graphene layer to generate electricity is actually genius. Imagine all sea liners getting electricity from their passage through the worlds oceans - simply breathtaking and quite plausible.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering

Simon Mitchell
absolutely and when i get a chance i will get back to it

author: @doomsdayedje
text:
I know this might sound a bit silly perhaps, but it is driving me mad.
It puzzles me what decides the polarity causing the potential-difference..
From what I understand, a single sheet covered with one medium (graphene) and left and right the same material cupper-strip is used.
So does direction flow of the salty water deside??
If I missed the explanation (in another video) I apologise for asking.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no worries - it doesn't have polarity - it is an AC generator

replies.author: @doomsdayedje
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith Well I'll be....I never would have thought that..
Thank you for answering the question.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: no worries

replies.author: @doomsdayedje
replies.text:
Robert Murray-Smith 

So is it a "safe" assumption that the ions make some sort of "seesaw" motion while sliding over the surface of the sheet.
Giving off some electron-voltage while going one way and adjusting the difference by picking electron-voltage from passing ions repeating the process over and over?
And Would covering the sheet with an ultra-thin layer of waterproof conducting salt(ink) prove it does the a seesaw-motion (doubt anything is strong enough to stop that motion on ionic level).. in stead of bouncing ions against the surface.
And does it then work with ordinairy tapwater? 
Loads of questions I still have, and you are right that it is a thing people should test themselves.
But since I am nowhere near as far as I see the things I see here, I'm sorry to have to ask.
I'll leave it with that before I become a troll...lol

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
doomsdayedje
it's more likely the ions 'stick' to the surface and impart a transient charge but are then dislodged due to movement of the water. The charge seperation is slow in the water and quick in the graphene which is why there is an overall usable charge

author: @vintasalo
text:
Great find...  I bought some graphene paint on ink and was wondering if a graphene coated copper plate would pull these ions out easier or more efficient, i should say.

One tesla patent was two copper plates, one suspended in the air and the other in the ground. Using this method with the graphene copper plates could produce some pretty interesting results. Can you paint the graphene on the copper plates and bake it on them?

author: @FredGandt
text:
I don't suppose trickling solution onto a sheet from a great height would yield _better_ results than the washing over method?
Sheet in a bucket on the floor, stand on a table, pour solution onto sheet gradually enough to be kinda splashy (fluid dynamics - something about chaotic energetic systems) :-)

Can the ions deplete in a closed system? I mean, if you cycled the solution for long enough, would it be less ionic?

P.S. I know all the technical terminologygigs ;-)

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: lol - mate i often get it wrong and have people continually writing to me to tell me so - some people take pleasure in it - basically I don't care - if the idea is put across that's all that matters - technobabble be damned. It's not a bad scheme but you would have to get the water up there in the first place and that takes energy - with the sea - and that's why I chose it - it's just laying around wobbeling about all of it's own - lol

replies.author: @FredGandt
replies.text:
Absolutely, although where I am for example (on the north devon coast), the tides reach 10 meters.
9 meter highs are frequent-ish along the south east coast.
That's quite a drop if one was to say put a collection tank atop a pole, and employ a valve and a system of floats to control when what did what.
Also tidal swell exerts truly astounding force that could easily shove water up well engineered tubes.

My actual point was more purely laboratory test conditions based; to test if "slamming" proved more effective than "washing".
For example then, we'd know that something like a box full of washboards like a beehive might be more effective than a smooth tube or sheet.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text:
Fred Gandt
then i would ay it is a definite possibility
